questions.question: Which country invented gunpowder?
questions.explanation: Gunpowder was invented in China by Taoist alchemists during the Tang Dynasty (9th century AD) while searching for an elixir of immortality. The formula spread along the Silk Road to the Middle East and eventually Europe, where it transformed warfare. The Chinese also invented fireworks.
questions.memory_hook: China: invented gunpowder while looking for immortality. Found explosions instead.

questions.question: Who was the South African president who served time in prison before becoming a symbol of freedom?
questions.explanation: Nelson Mandela was imprisoned for 27 years (1964–1990) on Robben Island and other prisons for his anti-apartheid activism. After his release, he negotiated the end of apartheid and became South Africa's first democratically elected president in 1994. He was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1993.
questions.memory_hook: Mandela: 27 years imprisoned, then president. From cell to chair of state.

questions.question: In which city were the first modern Olympic Games held?
questions.explanation: The first modern Olympic Games were held in Athens, Greece in 1896. Organized by Pierre de Coubertin, 241 athletes from 14 nations competed in 43 events. The Games were a revival of the ancient Greek Olympics, last held in 393 AD.
questions.memory_hook: Athens 1896 — where it all started, again.

questions.question: How long is a standard NBA game in minutes (regulation)?
questions.explanation: A standard NBA game consists of four quarters of 12 minutes each, for a total of 48 minutes of regulation play. NCAA college basketball uses two 20-minute halves (40 minutes total), and FIBA uses four 10-minute quarters (40 minutes total). Overtime periods in the NBA are 5 minutes each.
questions.memory_hook: NBA: 4 quarters x 12 minutes = 48. College: 2 halves x 20 = 40.

questions.question: Who sang 'Shape of You' and 'Bad Habits'?
questions.explanation: Ed Sheeran released 'Shape of You' in 2017 — it became one of the best-selling singles of all time with over 3.5 billion Spotify streams. 'Bad Habits' was released in 2021. Sheeran began his career busking on the streets of London and has become one of the world's best-selling music artists.
questions.memory_hook: Ed Sheeran: ginger, guitar, global domination. 'Shape of You' + 'Bad Habits.'

questions.question: In 'The Lion King,' what is the name of the villain lion?
questions.explanation: Scar (Taka) is the main villain in The Lion King (1994), voiced by Jeremy Irons. He kills his brother Mufasa and usurps the Pride Lands. The story is loosely based on Shakespeare's Hamlet. Scar is one of Disney's most memorable villains, known for his cunning and the song 'Be Prepared.'
questions.memory_hook: Scar has a scar. He killed Mufasa. 'Be Prepared' was his evil manifesto.

questions.question: Which album is considered the best-selling of all time?
questions.explanation: Michael Jackson's Thriller (1982) is the best-selling album of all time, with estimated sales of 66–70 million copies. It produced seven Billboard Top 10 singles and the iconic music video for 'Thriller,' directed by John Landis, which is the most-viewed music video in history on YouTube.
questions.memory_hook: Thriller: 70 million copies. The undisputed king of albums.

questions.question: Which French emperor was exiled to the island of St. Helena?
questions.explanation: Napoleon Bonaparte was exiled to the remote island of St. Helena in the South Atlantic Ocean after his final defeat at Waterloo in 1815. He lived there under British supervision until his death in 1821. It is one of the most remote inhabited islands in the world, specifically chosen to prevent his escape.
questions.memory_hook: Napoleon's last exile: St. Helena. So remote no one could rescue him.

questions.question: Which European country has the most UNESCO World Heritage Sites?
questions.explanation: Italy leads the world with 58 UNESCO World Heritage Sites, including the Colosseum, Pompeii, Venice, the Amalfi Coast, and the historic centers of Rome, Florence, and Siena. China is close behind with 57. Italy's extraordinary concentration of ancient history, Renaissance art, and natural beauty contributes to its record.
questions.memory_hook: Italy = most UNESCO World Heritage Sites in the world. 58 sites. Rome, Venice, Florence, Pompeii — the entire country is essentially a museum you can walk through.

questions.question: Which gas do plants primarily absorb during photosynthesis to make food?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb carbon dioxide (CO2) from the air through tiny pores called stomata in their leaves. Combined with water from the roots and energy from sunlight, they convert CO2 into glucose and release oxygen as a byproduct. This is why forests are called carbon sinks — they continuously pull CO2 out of the atmosphere.
questions.memory_hook: Plants breathe in CO2, breathe out oxygen. The exact opposite of animals. Trees are essentially CO2 processors — turning a greenhouse gas into wood and leaves while releasing the oxygen we breathe.

questions.question: Bhutan, the Himalayan kingdom nestled between India and China, measures national wellbeing using a unique metric it pioneered. What is it?
questions.explanation: Bhutan is the only country in the world that measures policy success through Gross National Happiness (GNH) rather than Gross Domestic Product (GDP). The concept was introduced by the fourth King of Bhutan in the 1970s and encompasses psychological wellbeing, cultural resilience, environmental conservation, and good governance. Bhutan is also notable for being carbon-negative (it absorbs more CO2 than it produces) and for being one of the last countries to get television (1999).
questions.memory_hook: Bhutan: Gross National Happiness instead of GDP. The only country measuring wellbeing as official policy. Carbon-negative — it absorbs more CO2 than it emits. TV only arrived in 1999. A tiny Himalayan kingdom between India and China that's doing things very differently.

questions.question: Gregor Mendel's pea plant experiments revealed that some traits are 'dominant' and some 'recessive.' What does it mean for a trait to be recessive?
options: It can only be inherited from the mother
It only appears when an organism inherits two copies of the recessive allele
It disappears from the population over time
questions.answer: It only appears when an organism inherits two copies of the recessive allele
questions.explanation: A recessive trait is only expressed when an organism is homozygous for the recessive allele (inherits one copy from each parent). A single dominant allele 'overrides' the recessive one. Mendel's key insight (published 1866, ignored for 35 years) was that traits don't blend — they are inherited as discrete particles (genes) with dominant/recessive relationships. Common recessive traits in humans include blue eyes, blood type O, cystic fibrosis, and albinism.
questions.memory_hook: Recessive = only shows when you have TWO copies. One dominant allele silences it. Mendel figured this out with peas — published in 1866, ignored for 35 years, then rediscovered. Blue eyes, blood type O, cystic fibrosis are all recessive. You can carry a trait invisibly for generations.
